desc: Ha Giang is Vietnam's most dramatic northern highland loop, where karst passes, minority villages, and deep river canyons create one of Southeast Asia's standout road-trip routes. Conditions vary strongly by season, with spring/autumn generally best for visibility and road reliability while monsoon months increase landslide and riding-risk exposure.
hls: Best riding in spring and autumn

todo.name: Ha Giang Loop (2–4 days)
todo.desc: Ride or hire an easy-rider across passes, karst valleys, and remote towns.

todo.name: Ma Pi Leng Pass viewpoints
todo.desc: Stop often for canyon and river panoramas in clear-weather windows.

todo.name: Dong Van old quarter night
todo.desc: Stay overnight for quieter mornings and weekend market energy.

todo.name: Lung Cu Flag Tower
todo.desc: Visit Vietnam's northern marker with wide mountain views.

todo.name: Ethnic market day
todo.desc: Plan around weekly market calendars in Meo Vac or Yen Minh.

todo.name: Nho Que boat segment
todo.desc: Add a short river boat ride if water levels and weather permit.

wifi.note: Reliable in Ha Giang City and larger loop towns, but patchy in remote mountain sections.

prac.fltNote: There is no commercial airport in Ha Giang. Fly to Hanoi Noi Bai (HAN), then continue roughly 280–300 km by limousine van/private car (about 6–8 hours) or overnight bus before starting the loop.
prac.lang: Vietnamese; English limited outside tourism hubs
prac.visa: Vietnam eVisa is available for many nationalities; confirm current duration and valid entry-point rules before travel.
alerts: Road conditions can change quickly in wet months due to rain, fog, and occasional landslides.
If self-riding, ensure you have valid licensing/insurance and route experience for mountain roads.
Fuel and cash access is limited in remote stretches — plan refuel and ATM stops in advance.

sweet: September to November and March to April are best for dry roads, views, and comfortable loop riding.
